faqs.question: What are the most popular Mean Well power supply series?
faqs.answer: Mean Well's most popular series include the LRS series for cost-effective enclosed power supplies (35-600W), the RS series for high-performance industrial applications (15-150W), the HLG series for outdoor LED lighting (40-600W), and the RSP series for high-power industrial applications (75-500W). For medical applications, the GSM and RPS series are widely used. The SD series offers reliable DIN rail mount power supplies for industrial control panels. Each series is optimized for specific applications with appropriate features, certifications, and price points. Our sales team can help you select the best series for your requirements based on power rating, environmental conditions, and certification needs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our sales team to discuss which Mean Well series best fits your application requirements and budget.

faqs.question: How do I cross-reference Mean Well part numbers?
faqs.answer: Mean Well uses a systematic part numbering convention that indicates series, power rating, and output voltage. For example, in LRS-350-24: LRS is the series, 350 indicates 350W power rating, and 24 indicates 24V output. The HLG-240H-48A indicates HLG series, 240W, 48V output, high input voltage range (H), and adjustable current (A). When cross-referencing competitor products, compare input voltage range, output voltage/current, power rating, efficiency, dimensions, and safety certifications. Our FAE team can assist with cross-referencing and provide comparison datasheets. We maintain a cross-reference database for many common competitor models to simplify the transition to Mean Well products.
faqs.decisionGuide: Provide your current part number to our FAE team for Mean Well cross-reference recommendations with full specification comparison.

faqs.question: What is the difference between constant voltage and constant current LED drivers?
faqs.answer: Constant voltage (CV) LED drivers maintain a fixed output voltage (typically 12V or 24V) and the current varies based on the LED load. They're suitable for LED strips and modules with built-in current regulation. Constant current (CC) drivers maintain a fixed output current (e.g., 700mA, 1050mA) and the voltage varies to maintain that current. CC drivers are required for high-power LEDs and fixtures without internal regulation. Mean Well offers both types: the LPV and LPF series are constant voltage, while the HLG and XLG series offer both CV and CC versions. Some drivers like the HLG series feature dual mode operation, automatically switching between CV and CC depending on the load characteristics.
faqs.decisionGuide: Check your LED specifications - if they specify a voltage range, use CV drivers. If they specify current (mA), use CC drivers. Contact us for assistance.

faqs.question: Can Mean Well power supplies be connected in parallel for higher power?
faqs.answer: Many Mean Well power supplies support parallel operation for increased power capacity or redundancy. Series with built-in parallel capability include the RSP-750/1000/1500/2000/3000, RST-5000/7500/15000, and DRP series. These models feature active current sharing that automatically balances load between units. For redundancy applications, the DR-RDN20 or DR-RDN40 redundancy modules can be used with compatible power supplies. When paralleling power supplies, use identical models with the same output voltage, connect the parallel communication wires as specified in the datasheet, and ensure adequate airflow for all units. Not all Mean Well models support parallel operation - check the datasheet for 'Parallel Operation' or 'Current Sharing' specifications. Our FAE team can design parallel configurations for high-power or critical applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: For parallel operation, select models specifically designed for current sharing. Contact our FAE team for redundancy and high-power system design assistance.

faqs.question: What safety certifications do Mean Well power supplies carry?
faqs.answer: Mean Well power supplies carry comprehensive global safety certifications. Standard industrial models typically include UL/cUL (North America), CE/TUV (Europe), CB Scheme (international), and CCC (China). Medical power supplies add IEC 60601-1 and ANSI/AAMI ES60601-1 certifications. LED drivers often include ENEC and EN 61347 certifications specific to lighting. Railway-certified models carry EN 50155 and AREMA certifications. Marine models have DNV/GL or ABS approvals. Hazardous location models carry ATEX and Class I Division 2 certifications. The specific certifications vary by product series, so always verify the datasheet for your specific model. As an authorized distributor, we can provide complete certification packages including test reports and certificates for your compliance documentation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Identify the certifications required for your target markets. Contact us for certification documentation and compliance support.

faqs.question: What is the difference between LRS and RSP series power supplies?
faqs.answer: The LRS series is Mean Well's cost-effective enclosed power supply line (35-600W) designed for general industrial and commercial applications. It offers good efficiency (up to 89%), universal AC input, and basic protection features at competitive pricing. The RSP series (75-3000W) is a high-performance industrial power supply with active PFC (Power Factor Correction), higher efficiency (up to 94%), and built-in parallel operation capability for redundancy or higher power applications. RSP series also offers wider operating temperature ranges and enhanced surge protection. Choose LRS for cost-sensitive applications with standard requirements, and RSP for high-performance, mission-critical, or parallel operation applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select LRS for standard applications under 600W. Choose RSP for high power, parallel operation, or enhanced performance requirements.

faqs.question: What is Power Factor Correction (PFC) and when do I need it?
faqs.answer: Power Factor Correction (PFC) is a feature that improves the power factor of AC input, reducing harmonic distortion and improving energy efficiency. Active PFC (APFC) uses a boost converter to achieve power factor >0.95, while passive PFC uses inductors for simpler but less effective correction (>0.7). PFC is required for CE compliance in Europe for power supplies above 75W, and recommended for any application where multiple power supplies share electrical infrastructure to prevent harmonic pollution. Mean Well's RSP series includes active PFC, while LRS series uses passive PFC on higher power models. PFC also reduces input current for the same output power, allowing smaller circuit breakers and wiring.
faqs.decisionGuide: For CE compliance above 75W or applications with multiple power supplies, select models with active PFC. Contact us for PFC requirements in your region.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the proper power supply size for my application?
faqs.answer: To size a power supply correctly, first calculate your maximum continuous load power requirement. Add 20-30% margin for peak demands, inrush currents, and future expansion. For example, if your maximum load is 200W, select a 250-300W power supply. Consider inrush current during startup - inductive loads like motors may draw 3-5x running current briefly. Check the power supply's peak load capability - many Mean Well models can deliver 120-150% of rated power for short durations. Factor in temperature derating - power supplies may need to be derated above 50°C ambient. For LED applications, account for LED driver efficiency in your calculations. When in doubt, consult our FAE team for sizing assistance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate your maximum load and add 30% margin. Contact our FAE team for complex loads or high-temperature applications.

faqs.question: What mounting options are available for enclosed power supplies?
faqs.answer: Mean Well enclosed power supplies offer flexible mounting options. Standard models include mounting tabs with slots for screw mounting to chassis or panels. The compact LRS series can be mounted in any orientation, while higher power models may require specific orientations for optimal cooling. DIN rail mounting adapters are available for many models to simplify installation in control panels. Some series offer chassis mount versions without the enclosure for integration into larger systems. Remote mounting is possible with appropriate wiring and ventilation. Always ensure adequate clearance (typically 50mm) around the power supply for airflow, and avoid mounting near heat-sensitive components. Refer to the mechanical drawings in the datasheet for detailed mounting dimensions and hole patterns.
faqs.decisionGuide: Standard screw mounting works for most applications. Consider DIN rail adapters for control panel installations. Contact us for custom mounting solutions.

faqs.question: What is the expected lifespan of Mean Well enclosed power supplies?
faqs.answer: Mean Well enclosed power supplies are designed for long service life with typical MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) ratings of 100,000 to 300,000 hours at 25°C, depending on the series. Actual lifespan depends on operating conditions, particularly temperature. The primary wear-out mechanism is electrolytic capacitor aging, which accelerates with temperature - every 10°C increase roughly halves capacitor life. Operating at 50°C instead of 25°C may reduce lifespan by 75%. Mean Well uses high-quality 105°C rated capacitors to maximize lifespan. Under typical industrial conditions (40°C ambient, 50% load), expected service life is 5-10 years. The 3-5 year warranty reflects the company's confidence in product reliability. For critical applications, consider operating below maximum ratings to extend lifespan.
faqs.decisionGuide: Design for moderate temperatures and loads to maximize lifespan. Use our MTBF calculator for reliability predictions in your specific conditions.

faqs.question: Can the LRS-350-24 be used for LED lighting applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, the LRS-350-24 is suitable for constant voltage LED lighting applications requiring 24VDC input. It can power LED strips, modules, and fixtures designed for 24V operation. The 14.6A output current supports up to 350W of LED load. For LED applications, ensure the power supply is sized with 20% margin above the total LED wattage to account for efficiency variations and ensure long life. The low ripple output (150mVpp) helps prevent LED flicker. For outdoor LED installations, consider the IP rating of the enclosure - the LRS series is IP20 rated for indoor use. For outdoor applications, use the HLG series which offers IP67 protection. The power supply's wide operating temperature range (-30°C to +70°C) accommodates most indoor lighting environments.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate total LED wattage and add 20% margin. For outdoor use, consider HLG series instead. Contact us for LED power supply sizing assistance.

faqs.question: What is the inrush current of the LRS-350-24 and how do I handle it?
faqs.answer: The LRS-350-24 has a maximum inrush current of 60A at 230VAC cold start, lasting approximately 2-3ms. This is typical for switching power supplies with large input capacitors. When selecting circuit breakers or fuses, use slow-blow (time-delay) types rated for at least 150% of normal operating current. For multiple power supplies on the same circuit, consider staggered startup or inrush current limiters to prevent nuisance tripping. The inrush current decreases significantly on warm starts. If inrush is a concern for your application, consider using a contactor with pre-charge circuit or select models with active inrush limiting. Our FAE team can help design appropriate input protection for your specific installation requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use slow-blow fuses or C/D curve circuit breakers. Contact us for input circuit design assistance for multiple power supplies.

faqs.question: How do I adjust the output voltage of the LRS-350-24?
faqs.answer: The LRS-350-24 has a fixed output voltage of 24VDC and does not include output voltage adjustment. The output voltage is factory set to 24V with ±1% tolerance. If your application requires voltage adjustment, consider the RSP series which includes a trim potentiometer for ±10% voltage adjustment. Alternatively, for small voltage adjustments (±5%), you may use external methods such as series diodes to drop voltage or a buck converter for more precise control. For LED applications requiring current control, use a constant current LED driver instead of a voltage source. If voltage adjustment is critical for your application, contact our FAE team to discuss alternative Mean Well models with this feature.
faqs.decisionGuide: For adjustable output, consider RSP series. For current control, use LED drivers. Contact us for application-specific recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the recommended derating for high temperature operation?
faqs.answer: The LRS-350-24 requires derating above 50°C ambient temperature. From 50°C to 70°C, output power should be derated linearly from 100% to 50% of rated power. For example, at 60°C ambient, maximum output power is approximately 280W (80% of 350W). Input voltage also affects derating - at low input voltage (85-100VAC), additional derating of 10-15% may be required above 50°C. For continuous operation at high temperatures, maintain at least 30% power margin below the derated rating to ensure long capacitor life. Ensure adequate airflow (natural convection or forced air) around the power supply. Mounting orientation affects cooling - horizontal mounting with terminals facing up provides best natural convection. Our thermal calculator can provide specific derating curves for your operating conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Derate 2% per degree above 50°C. Maintain 30% margin below derated rating. Contact us for thermal analysis of your installation.

faqs.question: Can multiple LRS-350-24 units be connected in parallel?
faqs.answer: The LRS-350-24 does not support direct parallel operation for current sharing. While you can connect multiple units in parallel for redundancy using isolation diodes, the units will not automatically share load current equally. For true parallel operation with current sharing, use the RSP series which includes built-in parallel capability. For redundancy applications with the LRS series, use external redundancy modules like the DR-RDN20 or DR-RDN40, or connect units with OR-ing diodes (such as RCD-48V). Each unit should be sized to handle the full load independently. When paralleling for redundancy, ensure both units have identical output voltage settings (measure and match within 0.1V). Contact our FAE team for redundancy system design assistance and wiring diagrams.
faqs.decisionGuide: For parallel operation, use RSP series. For redundancy with LRS, use external redundancy modules. Contact us for redundancy design support.

products.longDescription: The Mean Well RSP-750-48 represents the high-performance tier of Mean Well's enclosed power supply portfolio, delivering 750W of reliable power for demanding industrial applications. faqs.question: How do I connect multiple RSP-750-48 units in parallel?
faqs.answer: To parallel RSP-750-48 units for higher power or redundancy: 1) Connect all units' DC outputs in parallel with adequate wire gauge. 2) Connect the parallel communication wires (PC pins) between all units - these enable current sharing. 3) Ensure all units have identical output voltage settings (trim to within 0.1V). 4) Use common AC input or phase-staggered inputs to reduce inrush. 5) Up to 4 units (3000W total) can be paralleled with ±5% current sharing accuracy. For redundancy, size each unit to handle the full load independently. The DC OK signals can be wired in series for system monitoring. Always follow the parallel operation guidelines in the application note. Contact our FAE team for detailed wiring diagrams and system design assistance.
faqs.decisionGuide: For parallel operation, use identical units with matched output voltages. Contact us for system design and wiring diagrams.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of the DC OK signal on the RSP-750-48?
faqs.answer: The DC OK (Power Good) signal on the RSP-750-48 is a TTL-level status output that indicates when the output voltage is within regulation. The signal goes high (4-7V) when output voltage exceeds 90% of nominal, and low (<1V) when below 85% or during fault conditions. This signal is useful for: 1) System sequencing - delaying startup of downstream equipment until power is stable. 2) Monitoring - connecting to PLCs or monitoring systems for status indication. 3) Redundancy - wiring multiple DC OK signals in series for system-level fault detection. 4) Alarm systems - triggering alerts when power supply faults occur. The signal can sink up to 10mA. For isolated monitoring, use an optocoupler or relay. The DC OK signal provides early warning of impending failures, allowing graceful system shutdown before complete power loss.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use DC OK for system sequencing and monitoring. Wire in series for redundancy systems. Contact us for monitoring circuit design.

faqs.question: How do I adjust the output voltage of the RSP-750-48?
faqs.answer: The RSP-750-48 includes a trim potentiometer (VR1) accessible through the case that allows output voltage adjustment from 43.2V to 52.8V (±10% of nominal 48V). To adjust: 1) Remove power and wait for capacitors to discharge. 2) Locate the trim pot on the PCB (marked VR1). 3) Use a small screwdriver to rotate the pot - clockwise increases voltage, counter-clockwise decreases. 4) Apply power and measure output voltage under load. 5) Readjust as needed for precise setting. The adjustment is useful for: compensating cable voltage drops in distributed systems, matching battery float voltages, or optimizing for specific load requirements. When paralleling units, all units must be trimmed to within 0.1V for proper current sharing. Mark the final setting to prevent accidental adjustment. For applications requiring remote voltage adjustment, external circuits can be used.
faqs.decisionGuide: Adjust voltage to compensate for cable drops or match load requirements. Ensure all parallel units are matched within 0.1V.

faqs.question: What is the difference between active and passive PFC in power supplies?
faqs.answer: Active PFC (Power Factor Correction) uses a switching boost converter to achieve power factor >0.95, while passive PFC uses inductors for simpler but less effective correction (0.7-0.85). The RSP-750-48 uses active PFC, which offers several advantages: 1) Higher power factor (>0.95 vs 0.7-0.85) reduces apparent power and infrastructure loading. 2) Lower harmonic distortion (<20% THD vs >100%) meets CE EN61000-3-2 requirements. 3) Universal input voltage (85-264VAC) without manual switching. 4) Better efficiency across load range. 5) Lower input current for same output power. The trade-off is slightly higher cost and complexity. Active PFC is required for CE compliance above 75W and recommended for any application with multiple power supplies sharing electrical infrastructure. The RSP series' active PFC makes it ideal for European markets and high-reliability industrial applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: For CE compliance above 75W or multiple unit installations, select active PFC models like RSP series. Contact us for PFC requirements.

faqs.question: Can the RSP-750-48 be used for battery charging applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, the RSP-750-48 can be used for battery charging with appropriate external control circuitry. The 48V output is suitable for 48V battery systems (4 x 12V lead-acid or 15-16 LiFePO4 cells). For charging applications: 1) Use the voltage trim to set appropriate float voltage (54.0-54.8V for lead-acid, 54.4V typical). 2) Implement external current limiting as the RSP provides voltage regulation only. 3) Add temperature compensation for optimal battery life. 4) Use blocking diodes to prevent battery discharge back to supply. 5) Consider the RSP's current limiting behavior - it enters constant current mode during overload, which can work for bulk charging. For sophisticated charging profiles (bulk, absorption, float), external charge controllers are recommended. Mean Well also offers dedicated battery chargers (PB series) with built-in charging algorithms. Contact our FAE team for battery charging application design assistance.
faqs.decisionGuide: For simple charging, RSP with external control works. For sophisticated profiles, use dedicated PB series chargers. Contact us for charging system design.

categories.name: LED Drivers
categories.shortDescription: High-efficiency LED drivers for indoor, outdoor, and specialty lighting applications
categories.longDescription: Mean Well offers the industry's most comprehensive range of LED drivers, from indoor constant voltage models to outdoor IP67-rated constant current drivers. The HLG series is the flagship outdoor driver family, featuring high efficiency up to 96%, wide input voltage range (90-305VAC), and multiple dimming options. These drivers are designed for reliability in harsh outdoor environments with 7-year warranties and surge protection up to 6kV. As an authorized Mean Well distributor, BeiLuo provides expert LED driver selection assistance, dimming control design support, and application engineering for lighting projects of any scale.

selectionGuide.description: Use our LED driver selection guide to choose between constant voltage and constant current models based on your LED specifications and application requirements.

faqs.question: What is the difference between constant voltage and constant current LED drivers?
faqs.answer: Constant Voltage (CV) LED drivers maintain a fixed output voltage (typically 12V or 24V) while current varies based on the LED load. They're ideal for LED strips, modules, and arrays with built-in current regulation. CV drivers are simpler to design with and allow easy parallel connection of multiple LED strings. Constant Current (CC) drivers maintain a fixed output current (e.g., 350mA, 700mA, 1050mA) while voltage varies to maintain that current. CC drivers are required for high-power LEDs and fixtures without internal current regulation, providing optimal LED performance and lifespan. Mean Well's HLG series offers both CV and CC versions, with some models featuring dual-mode operation that automatically switches based on load. For LED applications, check your LED specifications - if they specify voltage range, use CV; if they specify current (mA), use CC.
faqs.decisionGuide: Check LED specifications - voltage range indicates CV, current (mA) indicates CC. Contact us for LED driver selection assistance.

faqs.question: What dimming options are available with Mean Well LED drivers?
faqs.answer: Mean Well LED drivers offer multiple dimming control options: 0-10V/1-10V analog dimming is the most common, compatible with standard lighting controls and occupancy sensors. PWM dimming (100Hz-3kHz) provides precise control and is suitable for digital control systems. DALI (Digital Addressable Lighting Interface) enables digital communication and individual fixture addressing for smart lighting systems. Timer dimming allows pre-programmed dimming schedules for energy savings. Resistance dimming (passive) uses a simple potentiometer for basic applications. The HLG series typically offers 0-10V and PWM as standard, with DALI versions available. Dimming range is typically 0-100% or 8-100% depending on model. For outdoor applications, ensure the dimming interface is properly sealed. Multiple drivers can be connected to a single dimming controller for zone control. Our FAE team can design dimming systems for projects of any complexity.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 0-10V for standard applications, DALI for smart lighting, PWM for digital control. Contact us for dimming system design.

faqs.question: What IP rating do I need for outdoor LED applications?
faqs.answer: IP (Ingress Protection) ratings indicate protection against solids and liquids. For outdoor LED applications, IP65 is the minimum recommended, protecting against dust and water jets. IP66 provides protection against powerful water jets, suitable for most outdoor installations. IP67 offers temporary submersion protection (up to 1m for 30 minutes), ideal for flood-prone areas or ground-mounted fixtures. IP68 provides continuous submersion protection for underwater applications. Mean Well's HLG series offers IP65 and IP67 options. Consider your specific environment: rain exposure needs IP65, washdown cleaning needs IP66, flood risk needs IP67. Also consider the installation location - ground-mounted fixtures need higher IP than wall-mounted. The driver enclosure must maintain its IP rating throughout the product life, so proper cable gland sealing is essential. For extreme environments, consider conformal coating on the PCB for additional protection.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use IP65 for standard outdoor, IP66 for washdown areas, IP67 for flood risk. Contact us for environmental protection recommendations.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the proper LED driver size?
faqs.answer: To size an LED driver correctly: For constant voltage drivers, calculate total LED wattage and add 20% margin. For example, 200W of LED load requires a 240W driver. For constant current drivers, sum the forward voltage drops of all LEDs in series (Vf total), then multiply by drive current to get power. Ensure the driver's voltage range encompasses your Vf total at all operating temperatures (Vf decreases as temperature increases). Check the driver's current rating matches or slightly exceeds your LED current requirement. Consider inrush current during startup - drivers should handle LED capacitance. For outdoor applications, derate 10-20% for high ambient temperatures. For dimming applications, ensure the driver supports your dimming range and control type. When in doubt, contact our FAE team with your LED specifications for proper driver selection.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate LED power and add 20% margin. For CC drivers, verify voltage range covers LED Vf. Contact us for driver sizing assistance.

faqs.question: What surge protection is built into Mean Well LED drivers?
faqs.answer: Mean Well LED drivers include comprehensive surge protection to withstand voltage transients common in outdoor installations. The HLG series features 6kV/4kA surge protection (IEC 61000-4-5 Level 4) on the AC input, protecting against lightning-induced surges and switching transients. This high level of protection eliminates the need for external surge protective devices (SPDs) in most installations. The protection is designed for common mode (line-to-ground) and differential mode (line-to-line) surges. For areas with extreme lightning activity or long cable runs (>100m), additional external SPDs may be recommended. The surge protection is self-recovering - after a surge event, the driver automatically resumes normal operation if undamaged. Mean Well's surge protection design has been validated through extensive testing and field deployment in harsh environments. The 7-year warranty on HLG series reflects confidence in this protection design.
faqs.decisionGuide: HLG series 6kV surge protection is sufficient for most applications. For extreme lightning areas, consider additional SPDs. Contact us for surge protection design.

faqs.question: How do I wire the dimming control for the HLG-240H-48A?
faqs.answer: The HLG-240H-48A offers three dimming input methods on dedicated wires: 1) 0-10V analog: Connect dimming+ (purple) to 0-10V control signal, dimming- (grey) to control ground. 0V = minimum, 10V = maximum brightness. 2) PWM: Apply 10V PWM signal (100Hz-3kHz) between dimming+ and dimming-. Duty cycle controls brightness. 3) Resistance: Connect potentiometer (100kΩ) between dimming+ and dimming-, wiper to dimming+. All methods provide 0-100% dimming range. The dimming circuit is isolated from AC input. For multiple drivers, parallel the dimming inputs - they draw minimal current (<0.5mA). Use shielded cable for long dimming runs (>10m) to prevent interference. The driver also supports timer dimming via internal DIP switches for simple scheduled dimming without external controllers. Always follow wiring diagrams in the application note for proper installation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 0-10V for standard controls, PWM for digital systems, resistance for simple manual control. Contact us for dimming system design.

faqs.question: What is the case temperature (Tc) and how do I measure it?
faqs.answer: Case temperature (Tc) is the temperature at a specific measurement point on the driver case, typically the hottest spot near the center. The HLG-240H-48A is rated for Tc up to 90°C, with 50,000 hour lifespan at Tc=75°C. To measure Tc: 1) Attach a thermocouple to the case center using thermal epoxy or tape. 2) Operate the driver at full load in the installed environment. 3) Allow 30-60 minutes for thermal stabilization. 4) Record the steady-state temperature. To keep Tc below 75°C for maximum lifespan: Ensure adequate free air space around the driver (50mm minimum), mount on thermally conductive surface if possible, avoid direct sunlight on black case, and consider derating or forced air for high ambient temperatures. The Tc rating is more critical than ambient temperature - a driver can operate at 50°C ambient but exceed Tc if poorly mounted.
faqs.decisionGuide: Measure Tc during prototype testing. Design for Tc <75°C for maximum lifespan. Contact us for thermal design assistance.

faqs.question: Can I use the HLG-240H-48A for indoor applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, the HLG-240H-48A can be used indoors, though it may be over-specified for some applications. The IP67 protection and 6kV surge protection provide excellent reliability even in indoor industrial environments. Benefits for indoor use include: High efficiency reduces cooling load in enclosed fixtures, 7-year warranty provides long-term peace of mind, multiple dimming options support advanced lighting controls, and robust design handles industrial power quality issues. Considerations: The IP67 housing is larger than IP20 indoor drivers, the premium price may not be justified for benign environments, and the black case absorbs more heat in direct sunlight (for skylight applications). For standard indoor commercial lighting, the LPF or XLG series may offer better value. For industrial indoor, high-bay, or harsh indoor environments, the HLG series is an excellent choice. Contact our FAE team for indoor driver recommendations based on your specific environment.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use HLG for harsh indoor environments. For standard commercial, consider LPF/XLG series. Contact us for indoor driver selection.

faqs.question: How does the timer dimming function work?
faqs.answer: The HLG-240H-48A includes built-in timer dimming controlled by DIP switches on the driver. Three timing profiles are available: 1) 3-hour: 100% for 3 hours, then dims to 50% (street lighting profile). 2) 5-hour: 100% for 5 hours, then dims to 50% (extended evening). 3) 7-hour: 100% for 7 hours, then dims to 50% (all-night applications). The timer starts when AC power is applied, so coordinate with photocell or timeclock control. The dimming transition is smooth over 1 minute to avoid noticeable changes. Timer dimming provides energy savings (20-30% typical) without external control systems. For more sophisticated scheduling (different profiles for weekends, seasonal adjustments), external 0-10V controllers are recommended. The timer function can be disabled via DIP switch for normal operation. This feature is particularly valuable for street lighting and area lighting where simple dusk-to-dawn dimming is desired.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use timer dimming for simple dusk-to-dawn applications. For complex schedules, use external 0-10V controllers. Contact us for dimming strategy consultation.

faqs.question: What maintenance is required for outdoor LED drivers?
faqs.answer: Mean Well HLG series drivers are designed for maintenance-free operation with 50,000+ hour lifespan. However, periodic inspection is recommended: Annually inspect for physical damage to case or cables, check cable gland tightness and seal integrity, clean accumulated dirt or debris from case fins (if present), verify mounting hardware is secure, and check for corrosion in coastal or chemical environments. The IP67 rating should be maintained throughout life - replace cable glands if damaged. The driver has no user-serviceable parts - do not open the sealed case. If failure occurs, replace the entire driver. With 7-year warranty and high reliability, maintenance costs are minimal. For critical applications, consider stocking spare drivers. The LED load typically has similar or shorter lifespan than the driver, so driver replacement usually coincides with LED replacement. Contact our FAE team for maintenance planning and spare parts recommendations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Annual visual inspection is sufficient. Replace driver if failure occurs - no field repair. Contact us for maintenance planning.

faqs.question: Can the LPF-60D-24 be used in damp locations?
faqs.answer: The LPF-60D-24 has an IP20 rating, which means it is suitable for indoor dry locations only. It is not rated for damp or wet locations without additional protection. For damp locations (bathrooms, covered outdoor areas), install the driver in a suitable NEMA-rated enclosure (NEMA 4X or equivalent) that provides the necessary moisture protection. Ensure the enclosure has adequate ventilation or cooling to prevent overheating. For wet locations (exposed to rain, washdown), use the HLG series which has IP67 protection. Alternatively, the LPF series can be used in fixtures rated for the location if the driver is contained within the fixture's sealed housing. Always check local electrical codes for specific requirements. Contact our FAE team for damp location installation guidance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use in dry locations only. For damp/wet, use enclosure or HLG series. Contact us for location-specific recommendations.

faqs.question: How many LED strips can I connect to the LPF-60D-24?
faqs.answer: The number of LED strips depends on the power consumption of each strip. The LPF-60D-24 provides 60W at 24V (2.5A). To calculate: 1) Check your LED strip power rating (Watts per meter). 2) Multiply by total length to get total power. 3) Ensure total is less than 60W (include 10% margin). For example, if your strip is 10W/meter, you can power up to 5.4 meters (60W / 10W/m = 6m, minus 10% margin = 5.4m). For multiple shorter strips, connect them in parallel to the driver output - ensure wire gauge is adequate for total current. Do not exceed 2.5A total output current. If you need to power more LEDs, use multiple drivers or upgrade to higher power model (LPF-90D-24). For long runs, consider voltage drop in the wiring - use appropriate wire gauge or position driver centrally. Contact our FAE team for LED system design assistance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate total LED power and ensure <54W (90% of 60W). Use multiple drivers for larger loads. Contact us for LED system design.

faqs.question: What wire gauge should I use with the LPF-60D-24?
faqs.answer: For the LPF-60D-24 (60W, 24V, 2.5A), recommended wire gauges are: AC input: 18 AWG (0.75mm²) minimum for up to 3m, 16 AWG (1.0mm²) for longer runs. DC output: 18 AWG (0.75mm²) minimum for up to 2m, 16 AWG (1.0mm²) for 2-5m, 14 AWG (1.5mm²) for >5m. Dimming control: 22 AWG (0.3mm²) is sufficient due to low current (<1mA). Always use copper wire rated for at least 300V. For DC output, consider voltage drop - at 2.5A, 18 AWG has ~0.04V drop per meter, which is acceptable for most LED applications. For longer runs, use larger gauge or position driver closer to LEDs. Use proper wire connectors rated for the current. Follow local electrical codes for installation. Contact our FAE team for wiring design assistance for complex installations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 18 AWG minimum for short runs, 16 AWG for longer runs. Contact us for wiring design for complex installations.

faqs.question: Can I use the LPF-60D-24 with smart home dimming systems?
faqs.answer: Yes, the LPF-60D-24 is compatible with many smart home dimming systems through its 3-in-1 dimming interface. For smart home integration: 1) 0-10V output: Many smart controllers provide 0-10V analog output - connect directly to dimming+ and dimming- wires. 2) PWM output: Some smart systems output PWM - connect to dimming input (check voltage level matches 10V). 3) Smart relays: Use on/off control without dimming by switching AC input. Popular systems like Lutron, Philips Hue (with controller), and various Z-Wave/Zigbee controllers work well. Ensure your smart controller can provide the required dimming signal type and voltage level. Some smart dimmers are designed for phase-cut dimming (TRIAC) and won't work with 0-10V drivers - verify compatibility. For integration with home automation platforms (SmartThings, Hubitat, Home Assistant), use compatible 0-10V output modules. Contact our FAE team for specific smart home system integration guidance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 0-10V compatible smart controllers. Verify dimming type compatibility before purchasing. Contact us for smart home integration.

faqs.question: What is the minimum dimming level of the LPF-60D-24?
faqs.answer: The LPF-60D-24 has a minimum dimming level of 8% (some specifications list 10%). This means the driver cannot dim LEDs completely to off - the minimum output is 8% of maximum brightness. This is typical for many LED drivers and is not noticeable in most applications. If you need true off (0%), use the AC input switching to turn the driver completely off. The 8% minimum is designed to maintain stable operation and prevent LED flicker at very low currents. For applications requiring dim-to-off, consider: 1) Using a relay or smart switch on AC input for off state. 2) Upgrading to HLG series which can dim to 0% (with some models). 3) Using a driver with built-in shut-off at minimum dimming. In practice, 8% is barely visible and most users won't notice the difference from off in normal use. The dimming curve is logarithmic, providing smooth perceived brightness adjustment from 8% to 100%.
faqs.decisionGuide: 8% minimum is normal for this driver. For true off, switch AC input. For 0-100% dimming, consider HLG series.

categories.longDescription: Mean Well's DC-DC converter portfolio includes isolated and non-isolated modules for distributed power architecture, battery-powered systems, and voltage conversion applications. The SD series offers reliable DIN rail mount DC-DC converters for industrial control panels, while the SPU and other series provide compact PCB mount solutions. selectionGuide.description: Use our DC-DC converter selection guide to choose between isolated and non-isolated converters based on your input voltage, output requirements, and isolation needs.

faqs.question: What is the difference between isolated and non-isolated DC-DC converters?
faqs.answer: Isolated DC-DC converters use a transformer to provide galvanic isolation between input and output, typically offering 1kV to 6kV isolation voltage. They prevent ground loops, protect against high voltage transients, and allow different ground references between input and output circuits. Isolated converters are required for medical applications, industrial systems with long cable runs, and safety-critical applications. Non-isolated converters (buck, boost, buck-boost) share a common ground between input and output, offering higher efficiency, smaller size, and lower cost. They're suitable for applications where input and output share the same ground reference, such as on-board voltage conversion within a system. Mean Well offers both types: SD series for isolated DIN rail applications, and various PCB mount series for both isolated and non-isolated needs. Choose isolated when safety, noise immunity, or ground separation is required; choose non-isolated for cost-sensitive applications with common grounding.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use isolated converters for safety, medical, or noise-sensitive applications. Use non-isolated for on-board conversion with common ground. Contact us for selection assistance.

faqs.question: What applications are DIN rail DC-DC converters used for?
faqs.answer: DIN rail DC-DC converters like Mean Well's SD series are primarily used in industrial control panels and automation systems. Common applications include: Converting 24VDC control voltage to 12V or 5V for sensors, PLCs, and HMIs; Providing isolated power for analog circuits to prevent ground loops and noise; Battery-powered systems requiring voltage conversion; Railway and transportation systems with 72V or 110V batteries needing 24V conversion; Telecommunications equipment requiring -48V to +24V conversion; Distributed power systems where different voltage levels are needed at various points. The DIN rail mounting allows easy installation in standard industrial enclosures alongside breakers, relays, and terminals. SD series converters offer 1.5kV isolation, making them suitable for most industrial isolation requirements. Their robust design handles the electrical noise common in industrial environments.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use DIN rail converters for industrial panels requiring voltage conversion or isolation. Contact us for panel design assistance.

faqs.question: How do I select the right isolation voltage for my application?
faqs.answer: Isolation voltage requirements depend on application safety standards and electrical environment. For basic industrial control with SELV circuits, 500V to 1kV functional isolation is typically sufficient. Systems requiring operator safety need 1.5kV to 3kV basic or reinforced isolation per IEC 60950/62368. Medical equipment requires 2xMOPP (Means of Patient Protection) with 4kV isolation per IEC 60601-1. High-voltage industrial systems or applications with long cable runs may need 5kV to 6kV isolation. Consider working voltage (continuous), transient overvoltage, and pollution degree of the environment. Mean Well's SD series offers 1.5kV isolation suitable for most industrial applications. For medical or high-voltage applications, select models with appropriate isolation ratings. When in doubt, specify higher isolation - the cost difference is small compared to safety certification failures. Contact our FAE team for isolation requirements specific to your application.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 1.5kV for standard industrial, 4kV for medical, 6kV for high voltage. Contact us for application-specific isolation requirements.

faqs.question: Can DC-DC converters be used in parallel for higher current?
faqs.answer: Most Mean Well DC-DC converters do not support direct parallel operation for current sharing. Unlike some AC-DC power supplies, DC-DC modules typically lack built-in current sharing circuitry. However, there are options for higher current applications: Use a single higher power converter rather than paralleling smaller ones; Use external load sharing controllers that balance current between modules; For battery charging or resistive loads, parallel connection may work without active sharing (each delivers according to its output voltage); Use redundant configurations with isolation diodes for critical applications. When paralleling is necessary, select converters with tight voltage tolerance (<±1%) and match output voltages carefully. Start with one converter and add others gradually while monitoring current distribution. For new designs, it's better to select a single appropriately-sized converter. Contact our FAE team for high-current DC-DC application design assistance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Avoid paralleling if possible - use single higher power unit. For redundancy, use isolation diodes. Contact us for high current designs.

faqs.question: What is the efficiency of Mean Well DC-DC converters?
faqs.answer: Mean Well DC-DC converter efficiency varies by series and operating conditions. The SD series DIN rail converters typically achieve 85-89% efficiency at full load. PCB mount converters range from 80% to 90% depending on topology and power level. Efficiency is generally highest at 50-100% of rated load and decreases at light loads. Isolated converters have slightly lower efficiency than non-isolated due to transformer losses. Switching frequency, MOSFET characteristics, and magnetic design all impact efficiency. For battery-powered applications, efficiency directly affects runtime - a 5% efficiency improvement can extend battery life significantly. At high loads, efficiency affects heat generation and thermal management requirements. When selecting converters, compare efficiency at your actual operating point, not just the headline number. Mean Well datasheets provide efficiency curves across load and input voltage ranges. Contact our FAE team for efficiency optimization in your specific application.
faqs.decisionGuide: Check efficiency at your operating point, not just maximum. Contact us for efficiency-critical application design.

faqs.question: Can the SD-50B-24 be used with 24V batteries?
faqs.answer: Yes, the SD-50B-24 is ideal for 24V battery applications. faqs.question: How does the isolation prevent ground loops?
faqs.answer: The SD-50B-24's 1.5kV galvanic isolation breaks the electrical connection between input and output grounds, preventing ground loops. In industrial systems, ground loops occur when: Multiple devices share ground connections through different paths; Long cable runs create voltage differences between ground points; Noisy equipment injects current into ground; Ground potential differences exist between building areas. The isolated converter allows the output to establish its own local ground reference, independent of the input ground. This prevents circulating currents that cause noise, measurement errors, and equipment damage. The 1.5kV rating ensures safety even if significant ground potential differences exist. For sensitive analog circuits, isolation is essential for accurate measurements. For PLCs and HMIs, isolation prevents communication errors caused by ground noise. The isolation transformer blocks both DC and AC common-mode noise between input and output.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use isolated converters when ground loops or noise are concerns. Contact us for ground loop troubleshooting and isolation design.

faqs.question: Can I parallel multiple SD-50B-24 units?
faqs.answer: The SD-50B-24 does not support direct parallel operation for current sharing. The units lack built-in current sharing circuitry, so connecting outputs in parallel will result in uneven current distribution - one unit may deliver most of the current while others deliver little. For applications requiring more than 50W: Use a single higher power converter (SD-100B-24 for 100W); Use multiple converters powering separate loads; For redundancy (not current sharing), use isolation diodes to OR outputs together, with each converter sized for full load. When using diodes for redundancy, ensure each converter has slightly higher output voltage setting to prevent back-feeding. For new designs, select appropriately-sized single converters rather than paralleling. Contact our FAE team for high-power or redundant DC-DC system design.
faqs.decisionGuide: Do not parallel for current sharing. Use higher power units or separate loads. Contact us for high power designs.

faqs.question: What wire size should I use with the SD-50B-24?
faqs.answer: For the SD-50B-24 (50W, 24V, 2.1A), recommended wire sizes are: DC Input: 18 AWG (0.75mm²) minimum for up to 3m, 16 AWG (1.0mm²) for longer runs. At 24V input, 2.6A draw is within 18 AWG capacity. DC Output: 18 AWG (0.75mm²) minimum for up to 3m, 16 AWG (1.0mm²) for 3-5m. At 2.1A output, voltage drop is minimal with 18 AWG. Ground: Same size as power wires. Use copper wire rated for at least 300V and appropriate temperature rating. For DIN rail installations, use ferrules on wire ends for reliable terminal connections. Keep input and output wire lengths as short as practical to minimize noise pickup and voltage drop. Separate input and output wiring to prevent capacitive coupling. Follow local electrical codes and panel wiring standards. Contact our FAE team for panel wiring design assistance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 18 AWG minimum for short runs, 16 AWG for longer runs. Use ferrules for DIN rail terminals. Contact us for panel wiring design.

faqs.question: What is the difference between SD-50B-24 and SD-50C-24?
faqs.answer: The SD-50B-24 and SD-50C-24 are both 50W isolated DC-DC converters but designed for different input voltage ranges. SD-50B-24: 19-36VDC input range, designed for 24V nominal systems (batteries, industrial control). SD-50C-24: 36-72VDC input range, designed for 48V nominal systems (telecom, higher voltage batteries). Both provide 24VDC output at 2.1A with 1.5kV isolation. The B version is for standard 24V industrial applications, while the C version is for 48V telecom or higher voltage DC systems. Select based on your available DC input voltage. If you have 24V, use B version; if you have 48V, use C version. Both have identical output specifications, efficiency, and protection features. The physical size and DIN rail mounting are the same. Contact our FAE team if you're unsure which version suits your application.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use B version for 24V systems, C version for 48V systems. Contact us for voltage selection guidance.

faqs.question: Can the SD-100C-12 be used with -48V telecom systems?
faqs.answer: Yes, the SD-100C-12 can be used with telecom -48V systems with proper wiring. Telecom -48V systems have the positive terminal grounded and negative at -48V. To use with SD-100C-12: Connect the telecom negative (-48V) to the converter's VIN+ (positive input). Connect the telecom ground (0V) to the converter's VIN- (negative input). This effectively provides +48V to the converter. The converter's isolation maintains output isolation from the telecom ground. Ensure the converter's input voltage rating (-36 to -72V equivalent) covers your telecom voltage range. The 1.5kV isolation protects sensitive 12V circuits from telecom ground noise. For central office applications, verify compliance with telecom safety standards. Contact our FAE team for telecom application design assistance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Reverse input connections for -48V telecom systems. Verify voltage range compatibility. Contact us for telecom design support.

faqs.question: How much heat does the SD-100C-12 generate?
faqs.answer: The SD-100C-12 generates approximately 12W of heat at full load (100W output / 89% efficiency = 112W input, 112W - 100W = 12W heat). This heat must be dissipated to maintain reliable operation. Thermal management considerations: Ensure adequate free air space around the converter (50mm clearance recommended). Mount on metal DIN rail or panel for heat conduction. Avoid mounting above heat-sensitive components. Consider panel ventilation or fans for multiple high-power converters. At 60°C ambient, derate to 80% load (80W) to keep internal temperatures within limits. The converter has thermal protection that shuts down if overheated, with automatic recovery when cooled. For thermally challenging installations, consider using multiple lower-power converters distributed across the panel. Contact our FAE team for thermal design assistance for high-density panel layouts.
faqs.decisionGuide: Provide 50mm clearance for airflow. Consider derating above 60°C ambient. Contact us for thermal design assistance.

faqs.question: What is the inrush current of the SD-100C-12?
faqs.answer: The SD-100C-12 has a maximum inrush current of 25A at 48V cold start, lasting approximately 2-3ms. This inrush is due to input capacitor charging and is typical for switching converters. When designing the 48V input circuit: Size fuses or circuit breakers for at least 150% of steady-state current (3.2A x 1.5 = 4.8A, use 5A minimum). Use slow-blow fuses or C/D curve breakers to tolerate inrush. For multiple converters, consider staggered startup or inrush limiters. The inrush is lower on warm starts after capacitors are charged. If inrush causes nuisance tripping, consider: Using higher capacity input source, adding inrush current limiter, or selecting power supplies with active inrush limiting. For battery-powered systems, ensure battery and wiring can handle the brief inrush without excessive voltage drop. Contact our FAE team for input circuit design for multiple converters.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use slow-blow fuses rated 150% above steady-state current. Contact us for input protection design.

faqs.question: Can I use the SD-100C-12 in a redundant configuration?
faqs.answer: The SD-100C-12 can be used in N+1 redundant configurations with external isolation diodes. For redundancy: Use two SD-100C-12 converters, each sized for 100% of load. Connect outputs through Schottky diodes (or redundancy modules) to a common bus. Set one converter output voltage slightly higher (0.2-0.5V) than the other. The higher voltage unit carries the load; if it fails, the second unit automatically takes over. Monitor both converters' DC OK signals for failure detection. Diode voltage drop (0.3-0.5V) reduces output voltage slightly. Size diodes for full load current with adequate heat sinking. This provides true redundancy without interruption on failure. For critical applications, consider using Mean Well's DR-RDN redundancy modules which simplify wiring and provide status monitoring. Contact our FAE team for redundant power system design.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use isolation diodes or redundancy modules for N+1 configuration. Size each converter for 100% load. Contact us for redundancy design.

faqs.question: What is the difference between SD-100C-12 and SD-100B-12?
faqs.answer: The SD-100C-12 and SD-100B-12 are both 100W 12V output DC-DC converters but designed for different input voltage ranges. Price is typically similar between versions. Contact our FAE team if you're unsure which input voltage range suits your application.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use C version for 48V systems, B version for 24V systems. Contact us for voltage selection guidance.

faqs.question: What is 2xMOPP isolation and why is it important for medical devices?
faqs.answer: MOPP stands for Means of Patient Protection, referring to isolation barriers that protect patients from electrical shock. 2xMOPP means two independent protection barriers between mains voltage and patient-connected circuits, providing redundancy if one barrier fails. This is the highest isolation level required for medical devices with patient contact (Type BF and CF applications). 2xMOPP typically requires 4kV isolation voltage and 8mm creepage/clearance distances. Mean Well's medical power supplies are designed with 2xMOPP isolation to meet the most stringent medical safety requirements. This ensures that even in single-fault conditions, patients remain protected from mains voltage. For home healthcare and body-worn devices, 2xMOPP provides the safety margin needed for unsupervised patient use. When selecting medical power supplies, verify the 2xMOPP rating in the safety certification documentation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 2xMOPP rated supplies for patient-contact medical devices. Verify certification documentation. Contact us for medical safety guidance.

faqs.question: What is leakage current and why does it matter in medical applications?
faqs.answer: Leakage current is the small current that flows through isolation barriers due to capacitive coupling and insulation resistance. In medical applications, leakage current must be strictly limited to prevent patient shock, especially for devices with patient connections (ECG electrodes, infusion pumps, etc.). IEC 60601-1 specifies maximum leakage currents: Normal condition: <100μA for Type B/BF, <10μA for Type CF. Single fault condition: <500μA for Type B/BF, <50μA for Type CF. Mean Well medical power supplies are designed with low leakage current (<100μA typical), meeting requirements for all medical device types. Low leakage is achieved through optimized transformer design, shielding, and Y-capacitor selection. When designing medical devices, total system leakage (power supply plus device) must be considered. Contact our FAE team for leakage current testing and compliance verification.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select supplies with leakage <100μA for medical applications. Test total system leakage. Contact us for compliance verification.

faqs.question: What is the difference between open frame and enclosed medical power supplies?
faqs.answer: Open frame power supplies like the RPS-200-24-C are bare PCB assemblies designed for integration inside medical equipment enclosures. They require the equipment manufacturer to provide enclosure, cooling, and terminal protection. Benefits include: Compact size, lower cost, flexible mounting, and integration with equipment cooling. Enclosed power supplies come in metal or plastic cases with all safety features built-in, suitable for standalone use. The RPS series (open frame) is ideal for OEMs integrating power into their equipment design. The MPM series (enclosed) is better for standalone applications or when the power supply needs to be user-accessible. Both provide the same 2xMOPP isolation and medical certifications. Route DC output wiring away from AC input to minimize coupling. Ensure creepage and clearance distances are maintained in your enclosure design. The supply must be grounded to protective earth. Consider thermal management - the supply can reach 70°C case temperature. Include AC inlet filter if needed for EMC compliance. Document the integration in your risk management file.
